Reproduction study · deep reinforcement learning

Mixing memory with practice, and the day the recipe fell apart.

I rebuilt RLPD — a 2023 method for learning control online while reusing a fixed batch of old experience — from scratch in PyTorch. Then I pushed it onto a body the paper never tried, ran the ablations one guardrail at a time, and hit a result that argued against the whole premise. This is the write-up I wish I'd had going in.

Hopper
Walker2d
HalfCheetah

My trained policies, 245k steps each, rolling out in MuJoCo. Near the expert data they learned from.

Online-only ablation on Humanoid — it beat the full method

What I was testing

The claim I wanted to break

RLPD's pitch is almost annoyingly simple. You don't need a specialised offline-RL algorithm to make use of a dataset. Take ordinary off-policy SAC, pour the old data into the same pool as the fresh experience the agent collects, sample half from each, and bolt on three guardrails so the value estimates can't blow up once the agent starts exploring. That's it. The paper says it matches or beats the fancier methods.

I wanted to know three things my own way: does it hold up in a clean-room PyTorch reimplementation, on the current Minari v5 datasets rather than the old D4RL ones, on a single desktop GPU? And does it survive Humanoid — 348 observations, 17 actuators, the task the paper quietly left out?

Honest footnoteThis started as a three-person course project. I owned the data pipeline and the evaluation side — every number and figure here comes from runs I set up, read, and normalized. The account is first-person because the findings are mine to defend; the algorithm core was a shared build with my teammates.

The three guardrails

What actually makes it work

MIX

Symmetric sampling

Every training batch is half fresh online experience, half the fixed dataset. A dial, not a switch — and, as it turns out, the part that broke.

BOUND

LayerNorm on the critic

One line of normalization that keeps the value function from chasing its own tail on actions it has never seen. The single most load-bearing trick in the paper.

PUSH

Ensemble + high UTD

Ten critics instead of two, and twenty gradient updates per environment step. Squeeze more learning out of every transition the agent sees.

Locomotion, medium data

It reproduced — with a caveat about why

Three tasks, three methods, three seeds each, normalized so the Minari v5 expert dataset reads as 100 and a random policy as 0. RLPD landed in the high 70s to high 80s across the board. IQL, the proper offline method, matched it on final score. SACfD — the naive "just dump the data in one buffer" baseline RLPD argues against — trailed, badly on Walker2d.

RLPD IQL SACfD
0normalized return · Minari v5 expert = 100100

Read the spread, not just the height. RLPD's seed-to-seed variance was tiny — on Walker2d it was ±0.2. IQL matched the scores but swung across seeds (±6–18). So the honest headline isn't "RLPD wins," it's "RLPD is the boring, repeatable one," which for a method you'd actually deploy is the better property.

Where it broke — on purpose

A critic running away

The most instructive run was the one that failed. Strip the LayerNorm out and you get SACfD, and on Walker2d its value estimates didn't converge — they climbed. Mean Q walked up to about 85,000 while RLPD sat quietly at 545. That gap is the entire point of the paper, drawn in one picture: without something to bound out-of-distribution values, the critic invents optimism it can't cash, and the policy chases it off a cliff.

Mean critic Q over training on Walker2d (log-ish, schematic). Bounded vs. not.

Beyond the paper

Humanoid, where the honesty lives

The paper stops at the easy bodies. I ran all three methods on Humanoid at a million steps each, and the numbers got interesting for the wrong reasons.

0
IQL final — but see the asterisk
0
RLPD, learning online from scratch
NaN
SACfD, on 2 of 3 seeds — the critic went to infinity

IQL looks like a runaway winner at 70. It mostly isn't. IQL spends a million offline gradient steps before it touches the environment, so it starts the online phase already at 48. Those steps cost nothing on an environment-step axis, which is why its curve floats far above RLPD learning from a cold start. The fair reading is "pretraining pays off on a hard body," not "IQL is the better algorithm." I'd rather report the asterisk than the trophy.

The result that got me

I turned off the data, and it got better

Then I did the thing you're supposed to do: take RLPD apart on Humanoid, one component at a time, and measure what each is worth. Drop the LayerNorm and it diverges to NaN, same as SACfD — the guardrail is real. Cut the update ratio, shrink the ensemble, and it gets predictably worse. All as expected.

Except one. Set the mix to online-only — throw the offline data away entirely, keep the strong online learner — and it didn't get worse. It jumped to 28, well past the full 50/50 method. That contradicts the whole premise.

My first thought was that the medium-quality data was just weak. So I gave RLPD the best data I had — the expert Humanoid set — and ran it to a full million steps. It flatlined at 4. Not better than medium. If anything, worse.

On this body, the prior data wasn't a head start. It was a leash.

The likeliest reason: an expert Humanoid moves nothing like a policy learning from scratch, so forcing half of every batch onto those far-off transitions drags the value function away from where the agent actually is. Better data makes that gap wider, not narrower. It's a single seed on the expert run and I'd want two more before I'd carve it in stone, but the flat trajectory across a million steps makes a lucky seed unlikely.

What I took from it

The honest version

RLPD's story holds where it should: on locomotion, with data that's genuinely better than what the agent would stumble into on its own, the 50/50 mix is stable and repeatable, and the LayerNorm earns its keep on every failure I induced. That part reproduced cleanly.

But the premise has an edge. On a hard body with imperfect data, a strong online learner didn't need the crutch — it did better without it. The interesting research questions live at that boundary, and I only found it because I ran the ablation I assumed would be boring. That's the lesson I'm keeping: run the boring ablation.

All of it fit on one desktop GPU — a single RTX 5070, state-based MuJoCo, small MLPs. Every run logs its seed, dataset version, and git commit; every score traces back to a raw return I can renormalize without retraining. If you want to check my work, it's all there.

Colophon

Karan Anchan

Reproduction, Humanoid extension, and ablation study of RLPD (Ball et al., ICML 2023).
